data.title: 10 S&P stocks defying the downturn as 70% of the index trades below 200-day MA
data.description:
As Wall Street contends with market uncertainty, a deeper look at the benchmark S&P 500 (SP500) reveals a sobering trend. Of the 500 companies that make up the index, only 150 are currently trading above their respective 200-day moving averages which is a widely followed technical indicator that helps investors gauge long-term market momentum.

The disparity underscores growing investor caution amid mounting economic headwinds, including ongoing trade tensions and tariff concerns that have weighed heavily on equities in recent weeks.

Despite the market pullback, certain stocks have managed to defy the trend, maintaining strong technical footing and distancing themselves from the broader selloff. Below, Seeking Alpha spotlighted the 10 stocks inside the S&P 500 that currently sit the highest above their 200-day moving averages. 

No. 10: Mckesson Corporation (NYSE:MCK), 18.62% above 200-day MA. 

No. 9: T-Mobile US (NASDAQ:TMUS), 19.64% above 200-day MA.

No. 8: Netflix (NASDAQ:NFLX), 20.09% above 200-day MA.

No. 7: EQT Corp (NYSE:EQT), 20.10% above 200-day MA.

No. 6: CenterPoint Energy Inc (NYSE:CNP), 20.52% above 200-day MA.

No. 5:  AT&T (NYSE:T), 21.11% above 200-day MA.

No. 4: Take-Two Interactive Software (NASDAQ:TTWO), 21.51% above 200-day MA.

No. 3: Philip Morris International (NYSE:PM), 23.22% above 200-day MA.

No. 2: Verisign Inc. (NASDAQ:VRSN), 24.68% above 200-day MA. 

No. 1:  Palantir Technologies (NASDAQ:PLTR), 62.18% above 200-day MA.

Year-to-date price action: MCK +21.5%, TMUS +21.1%, NFLX +10.6%, EQT +9.7%, CNP +17.6%, T +20.9%, TTWO +16.3%, PM +32.7%, VRSN +20.9%, and PLTR +28.6%.

data.title: JPMorgan CEO Jamie Dimon sells stake worth $31.5M
data.description:
JPMorgan Chase (NYSE:JPM) CEO James Dimon has sold a stake worth $31.5M in the company, an April 14 filing by the banking giant said.

Dimon sold 133,639 shares of common stock for $235.6774 per share.

JPM stock closed 0.63% lower at $234.72 yesterday.

data.title: Citi credit card net charge-off rate jumps to above prepandemic mark in March
data.description:
Citigroup (NYSE:C) credit card trust's net charge-off rate gapped up to 3.00% in March from 2.28% in the previous month and 2.90% a year ago, the bank disclosed in a regulatory filing submitted Tuesday.

The latest print also surpasses the prepandemic level of 2.95% in March 2019.

Meanwhile, Citibank Credit Card Master Trust I's delinquency rate of 1.44% slipped from 1.47% both in February and a year ago. It also trails the 1.62% mark recorded five years earlier.

During the month, lending activity stalled M/M and pulled back 6.2% Y/Y to $21.2B, the Securities and Exchange Commission filing showed.

data.title: Stocks falter as cautious traders monitor tariff developments; banks climb on earnings
data.description:
Wall Street on Tuesday had inched lower in choppy trade, as a boost from positive bank earnings petered out amid more caution over developments surrounding tariffs. 

The benchmark S&P 500 index (SP500) was last -0.16% at 5,397.18 points in late afternoon trade. The tech-heavy Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) was -0.20% at 16,798.49 points, while the blue-chip Dow (DJI) was -0.24% at 40,428.71 points.

Of the 11 S&P sectors, seven were now in the red. Financials topped the gainers.

The sector was boosted by a +4.4% gain in Bank of America (BAC) and a +3.5% advance in Citi (C). The number two and number four U.S. bank by assets, respectively, delivered double-digit Y/Y percentage growth in first quarter net income. 

BofA top boss Brian Moynihan noted "resilience" in consumers who have continued to spend and maintain healthy credit quality. Meanwhile, Citi CEO Jane Fraser reckoned that after all the trade wars, "the U.S. will still be the world’s leading economy, and the dollar will remain the reserve currency."  

On the tariff front, Bloomberg News reported that the European Union (EU) and the U.S. had made little progress in trade negotiations, and that the EU expects the U.S. trade barriers to remain in place. Earlier, another Bloomberg report said China had directed its domestic airlines to stop taking delivery of new aircraft from U.S. planemaker Boeing (BA).

White House press secretary Karoline Leavitt in an afternoon press briefing said more than 15 trade deals were actively being considered, and that U.S. President Donald Trump wanted to personally sign off on them. On China, Leavitt quoted Trump as saying "the ball is in (the Asian nation's) court" in terms of making a trade deal.     

Traders have had to digest several - sometimes confusing - updates on tariffs since Friday, but the overall picture has been one of positivity, helping boost sentiment on Monday. 

"Monday’s broad-based gains...saw the domestically-focused, mid-cap Russell 2000 lead the other majors, closing 1.1% higher on the day. This fresh positivity followed on from some encouraging, if confusing, tariff comments from the Trump administration after Friday’s market close," David Morrison, senior market analyst at Trade Nation, said.  

"President Trump refined his reciprocal tariffs once again, this time by exempting smartphones and other electronics. He also made it clear that the administration was looking at semiconductors and pharmaceuticals as the next sectors for specific levies. But this news was tempered after he also said he was considering temporary exemptions to tariffs on imported vehicles and parts. This would take some immediate pressure off Canada and Mexico whose auto supply chains are integral to U.S. car manufacturers," Morrison added.

Over in the fixed-income markets, U.S. Treasury yields stabilized after a bruising bond selloff in longer-term maturities last week. The benchmark 10-year yield (US10Y) was last down 4 basis points to 4.32%. The shorter-end more rate-sensitive 2-year yield (US2Y) was down marginally to 3.83%.

For more, see how Treasury yields have done across the curve on the Seeking Alpha bond page.

data.title: Anthropic adds Research, Google Workspace to Claude; AI bot on track for voice assistant
data.description:
Anthropic has introduced Research and Google Workspace integration to its AI model Claude, and is also nearing the launch of a new voice assistant product for the AI chatbot.

Anthropic — which is backed by Amazon (NASDAQ:AMZN) and Alphabet's (NASDAQ:GOOGL) (NASDAQ:GOOG) Google — said that with Research Claude can search across both the internal work context and the web to help make decisions and take action faster than before.

The company said Claude operates agentically, conducting multiple searches that build on each other while deciding what to investigate next. Research provides high-quality, comprehensive answers in minutes.

Research is available in early beta for Max, Team, and Enterprise plans in the U.S., Japan, and Brazil.

Claude now integrates with Gmail and Calendar. The company said that by connecting Google Workspace, Claude can search emails, review documents, and see a user's calendar commitments, removing the need to manually upload files or repeatedly provide context about the work and schedule, according to the company.

Anthropic noted that besides Research and Google Workspace, Claude Enterprise administrators can activate cataloging to improve Claude’s retrieval quality and accuracy. When cataloging is enabled, Claude uses a specialized index of an organization’s documents to find the information a user needs.

Web search, which was launched in the U.S. in March, is now available in Brazil and Japan and is automatically enabled for paid plans in these regions.

The company added that Google Workspace integration is available in beta to all paid users in profile settings.

Separately, Anthropic is nearing the launch of a new voice assistant for  Claude, approximately a year after competitor OpenAI started launching a similar feature for ChatGPT users, Bloomberg News reported.

The new feature, known as “voice mode,” could be launched as soon as this April and would initially roll out on a limited basis, the report added, citing a person with knowledge of the matter. The launch plans could change.

Anthropic did not immediately respond to a request for comment from Seeking Alpha.

Currently, the plans include unveiling three voices called Airy, Mellow and British-accented Buttery, the report noted.

The planned feature was found in Anthropic’s code by app researcher M1Astra and shared with Bloomberg News, which confirmed the findings, the report noted.

AI voice products have also raised concerns about impersonating how other people talk. Last year, Microsoft (NASDAQ:MSFT)-backed OpenAI delayed releasing its voice assistant for several months to work through potential risks.

Anthropic competes with several heavyweights in the AI space, including Google's Gemini. Google's latest Gemini model is now in first place on most of the industry-standard benchmarks, according to Seeking Alpha Analyst Simple Investment Ideas. Amazon Web Services' reliance on Anthropic models is now a disadvantage, as Google's exclusive Gemini models lure enterprises to Google Cloud Platform, the analyst noted.

data.title: American Express credit card metrics stay strong in March
data.description:
American Express's (NYSE:AXP) credit card quality held strong in March, with both its delinquency rate and net charge-off rate staying low.

The company's March U.S. consumer card delinquency rate was 1.4%, unchanged from February and level with a year ago. And it's still below its pre-pandemic level of 1.5%. 

Its net write-off rate of 2.4% declined from 2.5% in February and edged up from 2.3% in March 2024. Again, it remained below the March 2019 level of 2.5%.

Lending activity increased both M/M and Y/Y, with loans outstanding of $90.1B, rising 2.6% from a month ago and 9.5% from March 2024.

American Express's (NYSE:AXP) U.S. small business card delinquency rate of 1.6% was unchanged from February and January. And its net write-off rate of 2.6% was unchanged from the prior month and compared with 2.5% in January. 

U.S. small business card loans of $31.2B increased from $30.2B in the previous month and $30.1B in January, the company said.

data.title: Johnson & Johnson projects $400M tariff impact in 2025
data.description:
Becoming one of the first pharma majors to quantify earnings impact from the Trump administration’s newly imposed tariffs, Johnson & Johnson (NYSE:JNJ) on Tuesday projected a $400M headwind attributed to tariffs in 2025.

Speaking at JNJ’s earnings call, CFO Joseph Wolk said that much of that impact mainly relates to trade levies that the company’s MedTech division is likely to face after President Donald Trump exempted pharmaceuticals from a raft of tariffs, he announced early this month.

His remarks followed J&J’s (NYSE:JNJ) decision to reaffirm its full-year earnings outlook while slightly increasing its sales guidance with its better-than-expected Q1 2025 results on Tuesday.

While Trump later announced a 90-day pause on tariffs and reduced the overall tariff rate to 10% for many countries except China, Wolk said that the company’s $400M tariff hit reflects some of the steel and aluminum tariffs that the president announced early this year.

“It includes the China tariffs as well as the China retaliatory tariff. And that is probably the most substantial out of all the tariffs in terms of that $400 million,” Wolk added.

Amid Trump’s oft-stated plans to boost domestic manufacturing through levies, JNJ’s CEO Joaquin Duato said tax policy, not tariffs, will be the best solution to lift the U.S. production of both MedTech and pharma goods.

He pointed to Trump’s 2017 tax reforms, which he said led to a substantial increase in investments allocated towards domestic manufacturing of MedTech and pharmaceutical goods.

data.title: Trump trade war could damage U.S. credibility, Jamie Dimon says
data.description:
JPMorgan Chase (NYSE:JPM) chief Jamie Dimon on Tuesday urged the U.S. and China to "engage" on a trade deal, as President Donald Trump's campaign to reshape global trade risks damaging the U.S.'s credibility, according to a media report.

In an interview with the Financial Times, Dimon highlighted the U.S.'s position as a "haven" based on its rule of law, prosperity, and economic and military prowess. But that could be jeopardized by the steep tariffs that Trump has threatened as he seeks to move more manufacturing back to the U.S.

"A lot of this uncertainty is challenging that a little bit," Dimon said in the interview. "So you’re going to be reading about this nonstop until, hopefully, these tariffs and trade wars settle down and go away, so people can say, 'I can rely on America.'"

That confidence in the U.S. as a "safe haven" was shaken last week when investors turned away from Treasuries, sending 10-year yields jumping the most in decades as the president's swings on trade policy and moves against independent regulators had some investors questioning the U.S.'s role as the world's leading market.

Dimon also encouraged the Trump administration to negotiate with allies, such as Europe, the U.K., Japan, South Korea, Australia, and the Philippines, to forge a strong economic relationship.

He also discussed a potential post-JPMorgan venture.

When DImon, who has led JPMorgan for almost 20 years as CEO, finally steps down from leading the largest Wall Street bank, he said, "there's a little media thing I may do," although he declined to provide any details. "It's something I think all the papers should be doing. But I'm not going to tell you what it is right now."

Dear readers: We recognize that politics often intersects with the financial news of the day, so we invite you to click here to join the separate political discussion.

data.title: Rent the Runway stocks up inventory in a strategic move
data.description:
Rent the Runway (NASDAQ:RENT) fell in afternoon trading after the retailer reported Q4 earnings. Revenue was up 0.8% year-over-year during the quarter that ended on January 31, despite a 5% decline in active and total subscribers. Rent the Runway's (NASDAQ:RENT) net loss was narrowed to $13.4 million vs. a loss of $24.8 million a year ago.

On the positive side, Rent the Runway (RENT) noted that it operated at close to breakeven levels of cash consumption, while maintaining a steady revenue base. "This was achieved through improvements in our cost structure, through our ability to purchase inventory on attractive terms, and through improvements in working capital," highlighted the company.

Rent the Runway (RENT) did not factor in tariffs into its guidance due to all the uncertainties. "We believe we are fortunate that we directly import a relatively small portion of inventory and have placed orders for the majority of our inventory receipts for fiscal year 2025."

During the conference call, Rent the Runway (RENT) management said that it believes that a substantial increase in inventory in fiscal year 2025 is required to improve customer satisfaction meaningfully, especially as the inventory will continue to build through the course of the year. CFO Siddharth Thacker said the good news is that Rent the Runway (RENT) can utilize that inventory over multiple years, and believes this year's inventory investment will continue to pay dividends beyond fiscal year 2025. 

Shares of Rent the Runway (RENT) were down 14.3% in afternoon trading to $4.62 vs. the 52-week range of $3.70 to $41.81.

data.title: S&P 500 forms ‘death cross’ for first time since 2022 amid market uncertainty
data.description:
On Monday, the S&P 500 (SP500) triggered a closely watched technical indicator known as the “death cross,” where the index’s shorter term 50-day moving average dipped below its longer-term 200-day moving average.

Typically, this cross is seen as a bearish signal as it reflects short-term weakness overtaking long-term momentum.

This is the first occurrence of a death cross in the benchmark S&P 500 since March 2022, a period that saw the index decline roughly 25% from its highs. However, market experts caution against assuming an automatic downturn. Historical data shows that only 46% of such patterns have preceded deeper losses, according to Reuters.

As of Tuesday, the S&P 500 trades at 5,424 and is lower on the year by a total of 8%. At the same time the index’s 50-day moving average hovers at 5,736 and the 200-day moving average sits at 5,753. See a below chart highlighting the key technical crossing:

For investors looking to track the overall movement of the S&P 500 (SP500), several index tracking ETFs and mutual funds may be worth additional analysis:

S&P 500 Funds: (MUTF:FXAIX), (MUTF:VFIAX), (MUTF:VFFSX), (MUTF:SWPPX), (NYSEARCA:SPY), (NYSEARCA:VOO), (NYSEARCA:IVV), (NYSEARCA:RSP), (NYSEARCA:SSO), (NYSEARCA:SH), (NYSEARCA:SDS) and (NYSEARCA:SPXU).
